steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The skill is Rhetorical Synthesis. The student wants to emphasize the role a misconception played in the naming of a place. The correct choice must show (1) what the misconception was and (2) that it directly caused the naming.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The novel Las sergas de Esplandián featured a fictional island known as California.: This describes the source of the name but does not mention any misconception held by the explorers. It does not explain the naming of the real place at all. ✗
  • Choice B — To the south of the US state of California lies Baja California ("Lower California"), originally called California after a fictional place.: This notes that Baja California was named after a fictional place, but it omits the specific misconception — that explorers believed the peninsula was an island — which is the causal role the question asks about. ✗
  • Choice C — In the sixteenth century, Spanish explorers learned of a peninsula off the west coast of Mexico and called it California.: This states that explorers named the peninsula California but gives no reason why; the misconception that drove the name is absent. ✗
  • Choice D — Thinking it was an island, Spanish explorers called a peninsula California after an island in a popular novel.: The participial phrase “Thinking it was an island” names the misconception, and the rest of the sentence shows it directly caused the naming of the peninsula. Both required elements are present. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
